title: About Shiloh Ridge Farm
triggers: about the farm|tell me about|shiloh ridge|what is this farm|what kind of farm|what do you raise|what do you farm
response: Shiloh Ridge Farm is a Katahdin sheep and livestock operation in Maitland, Missouri, run by Dominic Hanway. We raise registered Katahdin hair sheep alongside hogs, focusing on quality breeding stock, farm-raised meat products, and direct sales to families and buyers across the region.

title: Katahdin Sheep
triggers: katahdin|hair sheep|what breed|breed of sheep|no wool|shed their coat|parasite|khsi|registered sheep
response: Katahdin sheep are a low-maintenance hair breed — they naturally shed their coat every spring so there's no shearing needed. They're known for excellent meat quality, strong maternal instincts, and parasite resistance, which makes them well-suited for Missouri's climate. Our flock is KHSI-registered breeding stock.
suggestions: View Livestock|Learn More on Katahdin Page|Contact About Breeding Stock

title: Confused Visitor

title: Farm Values
triggers: farm values|your values|what do you believe|why farming|philosophy|mission|what matters to you|care about animals
response: We're a family farm that believes in raising animals the right way — on pasture, with good care, for families who want to know where their food comes from. Dominic runs Shiloh Ridge as both a livelihood and a responsibility.
suggestions: Tell Me About the Farm|Contact Us

title: Family Farm
triggers: family farm|small farm|who owns|who runs|is this a family operation|how big
response: Shiloh Ridge is a small family farm owned and operated by Dominic Hanway in Maitland, Missouri. It's a hands-on operation — not a commercial feedlot.
suggestions: Tell Me About the Farm|Contact Us

title: Direct Farm Sales
triggers: direct from farm|farm to table|buy direct|no middleman|why buy from farm|why not grocery store
response: Buying direct from Shiloh Ridge means you know exactly what you're getting — animals raised on our own farm, processed at an inspected facility, and delivered or picked up fresh. No grocery store middleman.

title: Why Buy Local Meat
triggers: why local|local meat|buy local|support local|local farm beef|local pork|local lamb|local food
response: Local farm-raised meat is fresher, traceable, and raised by someone you can actually call. You're supporting a Missouri family farm and getting a better product than what's on most store shelves.
suggestions: Go to Products|Tell Me About the Farm

title: What Farm-Raised Means
triggers: farm raised|pastured|what does farm raised mean|how are animals raised|natural|humane
response: Our animals are raised on our property in Maitland, Missouri — pasture access, good feed, and hands-on care. They're processed at an inspected facility and packaged for your freezer. That's what farm-raised means here.
suggestions: Go to Products|Tell Me About the Farm

title: Buyer Expectations
triggers: what to expect|buying from a farm|first time buyer|buying farm meat|expectations|what is the process
response: Buying from a farm is a little different from a grocery store. You'll select your animal or product, place an order or reserve, pay a deposit if applicable, then pick up or arrange delivery when it's ready. Dominic will walk you through everything.

title: Farm Visit Information
triggers: can i visit|visit the farm|come to the farm|on-farm visit|see the animals|farm tour|see your sheep
response: We welcome serious buyers, but farm visits are by appointment — please contact us before coming out so Dominic can be available to help you. Call or email ahead: (660) 254-6226 or shilohridgefarm@gmail.com.

title: Appointment Only
triggers: appointment|do i need an appointment|just show up|drop in|walk in
response: Yes — please contact us before visiting the farm. We're a working farm and Dominic isn't always available without notice. A quick call at (660) 254-6226 makes sure your trip is worth the drive.

title: Why Hair Sheep
triggers: why hair sheep|hair sheep benefits|why katahdin not wool|advantages of hair sheep|no fleece|no wool sheep
response: Hair sheep like Katahdins don't produce commercial wool — they shed naturally every spring. That means no shearing cost or labor, lower overhead, and more time focused on meat production and flock health.

title: No Shearing Needed
triggers: do you shear|do katahdins need shearing|shearing sheep|do they need haircuts|trim wool
response: Katahdins shed their own coats every spring — no shearing required. That's one of the big advantages of the breed. No shearing equipment, no shearing crew, no lanolin-covered fleece to deal with.

title: Parasite Resistance
triggers: parasite resistance|parasite resistant|worms|barber pole worm|haemonchus|deworming
response: Katahdins are known for above-average resistance to internal parasites, especially barber pole worm — a major issue in humid states like Missouri. We still monitor and manage flock health, but the breed's genetics are a real advantage here.
suggestions: Tell Me About Katahdins|Contact About Health Records

title: Heat Tolerance
triggers: heat tolerant|hot climate|summer heat|heat stress|do they handle heat|missouri heat
response: Katahdins handle Missouri summers well. Their hair coat (not heavy wool) and heat-tolerant genetics make them more comfortable in warm, humid conditions than many traditional wool breeds.

title: Maternal Ability
triggers: maternal instinct|mothering ability|do they take care of lambs|good mothers|ewe raising lambs|lamb survival
response: Katahdin ewes are excellent mothers — good milk production, attentive to their lambs, and tend to have low assistance requirements at lambing. Strong maternal traits are part of what we select for in our flock.

title: Lamb Meat Quality
triggers: katahdin meat quality|is lamb good|how does lamb taste|mild lamb|tender lamb|does katahdin lamb taste strong
response: Katahdin lamb is known for its mild, clean flavor — less gamey than some traditional wool breed lamb. The meat-focused genetics produce well-muscled carcasses with good yield and quality marbling.
suggestions: Tell Me About Katahdins|Go to Products

title: Low-Maintenance Breed
triggers: low maintenance sheep|easy sheep|beginner sheep breed|less work|easy to care for|self-sufficient|independent sheep
response: Katahdins are one of the lower-maintenance sheep breeds. No shearing, good parasite resistance, strong maternal instincts, and generally hardy in Missouri's climate. They're a practical choice for small farms and serious operations alike.

title: Hair vs Wool Sheep
triggers: difference between hair and wool|hair sheep vs wool sheep|katahdin vs merino|wool sheep comparison|do katahdins produce wool
response: The key difference: wool sheep grow heavy fleece that must be sheared annually, while hair sheep like Katahdins shed naturally. Hair breeds typically have lower overhead, better heat tolerance, and are primarily selected for meat production rather than fiber.

title: Katahdins for Small Farms
triggers: katahdin for small farm|good for beginners|small flock|homestead sheep|backyard sheep|first sheep
response: Katahdins are a popular choice for small farms and homesteads. Their low-maintenance nature, moderate size, good meat yield, and forgiving temperament make them practical for owners who are managing everything themselves.
suggestions: Tell Me About Katahdins|View Livestock|Contact Us

title: Buying Lambs
triggers: buy a lamb|purchase a lamb|how do i get a lamb|lamb for sale|market lamb
response: We have lambs available depending on the season. Check the Livestock page for current listings, or contact Dominic to ask about upcoming availability. You can also submit an inquiry directly from any listing.

title: Buying Rams
triggers: buy a ram|purchase a ram|breeding ram|stud ram|want a ram
response: Breeding rams are available seasonally from our KHSI-registered flock. Check the Livestock page for current listings with registration details, or contact us to ask about upcoming availability.

title: Buying Ewes
triggers: buy a ewe|purchase ewes|breeding ewe|female sheep|buy female sheep|ewe lambs
response: We sell Katahdin ewes and ewe lambs as breeding stock. Current listings are on the Livestock page with photos, pedigree info, and pricing. Contact Dominic to discuss specific needs for your flock.

title: Breeding Stock Explained
triggers: what is breeding stock|breeding quality|why buy registered|registered breeding stock|what makes breeding stock
response: Breeding stock animals are sold for reproductive purposes — building or improving a flock. Ours are KHSI-registered with documented pedigrees, health records, and selection traits like parasite resistance, muscling, and mothering ability on record.

title: Market Lamb Explained
triggers: market lamb|what is a market lamb|market weight lamb|show lamb|feeder lamb
response: A market lamb is raised for meat, typically sold at or near processing weight. We sell both breeding stock and market-weight lambs depending on current inventory. Check the Livestock page or contact us.

title: Starting a Flock
triggers: starter flock|starting out with sheep|first sheep|how many to start|beginning flock|starting a flock
response: For a starter flock, most beginners do well with two to five ewes and a proven ram to start. Katahdins are forgiving for new shepherds. Contact Dominic — he can help match animals to your setup and goals.

title: Ram Selection Basics
triggers: how to pick a ram|what to look for in a ram|choosing a ram|ram conformation|ram genetics
response: When selecting a ram, look at EPDs if available, body condition and muscling, scrotal circumference, teeth, feet, and temperament. Registration and documented pedigree tell you something about what he'll pass on. Contact Dominic to discuss what works for your flock.

title: Ewe Selection Basics
triggers: how to pick a ewe|choosing ewes|what to look for in a ewe|ewe quality|good ewe
response: Good ewes should have a strong maternal record (or come from one), good feet, sound udder structure, and a body type that shows muscle and condition without being overweight. Our ewes are selected from our own flock — ask about individual records.

title: Replacement Ewes
triggers: replacement ewe|replacement female|culling ewes|ewe lamb as replacement|new ewe for flock
response: Replacement ewes are young females kept or sold to maintain or improve a breeding flock. We select ours from our best-producing mothers. If you're looking for replacements, contact Dominic with your flock goals.

title: Bottle Lambs
triggers: bottle lamb|orphan lamb|rejected lamb|hand raised lamb|pet lamb
response: Bottle lambs come up occasionally on any farm. Contact Dominic if you're interested — availability isn't predictable and won't always be listed on the website.

title: Are Sheep Beginner Friendly
triggers: are sheep easy|can beginners raise sheep|is sheep farming hard|easy to raise|good for first time
response: Katahdin sheep are one of the more forgiving breeds for beginners — low maintenance, good instincts, and no shearing. That said, all livestock require proper fencing, water, feed, and basic health management. Happy to point you toward resources if you're just starting out.

title: What to Ask Before Buying Sheep
triggers: what questions to ask|what to ask the farmer|before buying sheep|due diligence|checklist before buying
response: Good questions before buying: What's the animal's health history? Is it registered and does it come with papers? How old? What's its parasite resistance history? Any prior lambing records for ewes? Dominic can answer all of these for any animal on our listing.

title: Preparing for Livestock Pickup
triggers: how to prepare for pickup|what do i need to pick up sheep|before i come get my animal|ready to pick up
response: Before pickup: confirm the date with Dominic, have a clean trailer with proper bedding, bring any required paperwork or payment balance. Have water and feed ready at your destination. First-timers — just ask, Dominic is happy to walk you through it.

title: Trailer and Transport
triggers: how to transport sheep|trailer for sheep|what trailer|haul sheep|livestock trailer
response: Sheep can be transported in a standard livestock or stock trailer with adequate ventilation and non-slip bedding. Even a small utility trailer works for a few animals. Avoid overloading and plan for water access on longer hauls. Contact us if you need guidance.

title: Livestock Reservation Process
triggers: reservation process|how to hold an animal|reserve before buying|put my name on it|how reservations work
response: To reserve an animal, contact Dominic directly — call (660) 254-6226 or email shilohridgefarm@gmail.com. He'll confirm availability and any deposit requirements. Reservations are on a first-come, first-served basis.

title: What Registered Means
triggers: what does registered mean|registered animal|what is a registered sheep|why does registration matter
response: A registered animal has a documented pedigree on file with a breed association (ours are KHSI — Katahdin Hair Sheep International). Registration confirms parentage, birth type, and breed purity. It's important for buyers building a serious breeding program.

title: KHSI Explained
triggers: khsi|katahdin hair sheep international|what is khsi|breed registry|breed association|sheep registry
response: KHSI stands for Katahdin Hair Sheep International — the official breed registry for Katahdin sheep. Registration through KHSI documents sire, dam, birth type, genotype, and performance data. Our flock is KHSI-registered.

title: Why Registration Papers Matter
triggers: why do papers matter|registration papers|why bother with papers|is registration important|worth the paperwork
response: Registration papers prove an animal's pedigree. For breeding stock buyers, papers let you verify what you're buying, enroll offspring in the registry, and build a documented flock over time. Unregistered animals can't produce registered offspring.
suggestions: Tell Me About KHSI|Contact About Breeding Stock

title: Ear Tags and IDs
triggers: ear tags|tag numbers|animal id|how are they identified|scrapie tags|what do the tags mean
response: All our animals carry identification tags. KHSI-registered sheep have their registration number and flock ID on file. We can provide full ID records with any purchase.

title: Flock Records
triggers: flock records|production records|do you keep records|breeding records|health history|documentation
response: We maintain individual animal records including birth type, sire and dam, registration number, health history, and production notes. Ask Dominic for records on any specific animal you're considering.

title: Transfer Paperwork
triggers: transfer paperwork|registration transfer|how to transfer papers|change of ownership|transfer registration
response: When you purchase a KHSI-registered animal, we provide the transfer documentation needed to register the animal in your name with KHSI. Ask Dominic — he handles the transfer process.

title: Breeding Records
triggers: breeding records|who is the sire|who is the dam|parentage|sire information|dam information
response: Each registered animal's record includes sire name and tag, dam name and tag, birth type (single/twin), and genotype where available. All of this is on the KHSI certificate and in our flock records.

title: Pedigree Questions
triggers: pedigree|bloodline|family history|ancestry|genetic background|what line
response: We can provide pedigree information for all registered animals. Contact Dominic directly for specifics — he knows the flock well and can walk you through any animal's background.

title: Basic Health Questions
triggers: is the animal healthy|health status|how healthy|health of your animals|well cared for
response: We maintain a healthy, managed flock — animals are monitored, vaccinated, and treated as needed. Specific health records for individual animals are available — ask Dominic when you're considering a purchase.

title: Vaccination Status
triggers: vaccinated|do you vaccinate|what vaccines|vaccination records|shots
response: Vaccination protocols vary by animal and season. Individual health records are available for animals you're considering purchasing. Contact Dominic for specifics.

title: Parasite Management
triggers: parasite management|how do you manage parasites|worming|fecal egg count|famacha|deworming protocol
response: We manage parasites using a targeted selective treatment approach — fecal egg count monitoring and FAMACHA scoring rather than blanket deworming. Katahdins' genetic resistance helps, but we stay on top of flock health actively.

title: Hoof Care
triggers: hoof care|hoof trimming|feet|footrot|do you trim hooves|foot health
response: Hoof care is part of routine flock management. We trim as needed and monitor for foot problems. Animals offered for sale are in good feet condition. Contact Dominic if you have specific concerns about a listing.

title: Mineral Needs
triggers: minerals|mineral supplementation|what minerals do sheep need|selenium|copper|salt blocks
response: Sheep have specific mineral needs — including selenium and copper at appropriate levels for the region. We provide appropriate mineral supplementation for our flock. Missouri soils can be selenium-deficient, which is something to plan for.

title: Shelter Requirements
triggers: shelter|barn|how much shelter|what kind of shelter|do sheep need a barn|sheep housing
response: Sheep need protection from wind, rain, and extreme cold — especially lambs. A three-sided run-in shed is often sufficient. Full barn housing is helpful but not always required. Your setup will depend on your climate and flock size.

title: Fencing for Sheep
triggers: fencing|what fence for sheep|sheep fence|how to fence|electric fence|woven wire
response: Sheep respect woven wire or high-tensile electric fencing. Barbed wire is not recommended. A 47-inch woven wire fence with one electric wire at the top is a common and effective setup. Guardian dogs change your fencing calculations — contact us if you're setting up for dogs too.

title: Water Requirements
triggers: water|how much water|do sheep need water|watering sheep|water trough|drinking water
response: Sheep need constant access to clean, fresh water — roughly a gallon per head per day, more in heat or when lactating. Automatic waterers or large troughs work well. Don't underestimate water needs in summer.

title: Quarantine New Animals
triggers: quarantine|new animals|biosecurity|introducing new sheep|biosecurity protocol|isolation period
response: Any new animals entering your flock should be quarantined for 30 days — this protects your existing herd from introducing disease or parasites. Keep new arrivals separate, monitor closely, and do a fecal test during the quarantine period.

title: Veterinary Questions
triggers: vet|veterinarian|do you have a vet|recommend a vet|health emergency|sick animal|veterinary care
response: For specific health questions or emergencies, always work with a licensed veterinarian. We're not a vet service and can't give medical diagnoses. We can share what we do on our farm, but your animals need a vet relationship for their care.

title: Animal Condition Questions
triggers: what condition|body condition|is the animal in good shape|fat score|thin sheep|skinny animal|over or underweight
response: Body condition scoring (BCS) on a 1–5 scale is how we assess condition — we target 2.5–3.5 for most animals. Ask Dominic about the BCS of specific animals you're considering. Photos and in-person inspection are always best before purchase.

title: Farm-Raised Lamb
triggers: what is farm raised lamb|your lamb|how is it different|local lamb|pasture raised lamb|fresh lamb
response: Our lamb comes from Katahdin sheep raised on our farm in Maitland, Missouri. They're processed at an inspected facility and packaged for your freezer. You're getting real, traceable farm-raised meat — not commodity product.

title: Farm-Raised Pork
triggers: what is farm raised pork|your pork|how is your pork raised|local pork|farm hog|fresh pork
response: We raise hogs on our farm and sell them as whole or half hog packages with custom cut options. Processed at an inspected facility, packaged fresh, and ready for your freezer.

title: Whole Hog Explained
triggers: what is a whole hog|whole hog order|what do i get with a whole hog|whole hog purchase|full hog
response: A whole hog order means you're buying the full animal, processed and cut to your specifications. You'll get all the major primal cuts — chops, shoulders, hams, bacon, ribs, sausage, and more. Ask Butch for a full weight and pricing breakdown.

title: Half Hog Explained
triggers: what is a half hog|half hog order|half of a hog|split hog|half pig
response: A half hog is exactly what it sounds like — half the animal, split lengthwise. You get a proportional share of all the cuts: chops, shoulder, ham, bacon, ribs, and sausage options. A good choice for smaller families or first-time buyers.

title: Whole Lamb Explained
triggers: what is a whole lamb|whole lamb order|buy a whole lamb|entire lamb
response: A whole lamb order gives you the full carcass processed to your cut preferences — leg roasts, loin chops, shoulder, rack, shanks, ground lamb, and more. Ask Butch for a weight estimate and pricing breakdown.

title: Butcher Cut Basics
triggers: what are cuts|what is a cut|how do cuts work|primal cuts|what cuts do i get
response: When you buy a whole or half animal, it gets cut into specific sections at the processor — chops, roasts, ground, ribs, and so on. You typically fill out a cut sheet telling the processor how you want it done. Butch can walk you through what's available.
suggestions: Bring in Butch|Calculate a whole hog

title: Custom Processing Explained
triggers: custom processing|custom cut|choose my cuts|cut sheet|custom order
response: Custom processing means you tell the butcher how you want your animal cut — thickness of chops, bone-in vs boneless, how to handle trim, sausage options, etc. We work with inspected processors and will walk you through the cut sheet process.

title: Hanging Weight Explained
triggers: hanging weight|what is hanging weight|carcass weight|hanging weight vs packaged|take-home weight
response: Hanging weight is the weight of the carcass after slaughter but before cutting and trimming. Your final take-home weight will be less — typically 65–75% of hanging weight depending on cut choices. Ask Butch for specific estimates.

title: Deposit Explained
triggers: deposit|down payment|how much deposit|why deposit|refundable deposit
response: Deposits are collected to hold your animal or order in the queue. They confirm your order is serious and help us plan processing schedules. Contact Dominic for current deposit requirements on specific orders.

title: Pickup Process
triggers: how does pickup work|where do i pick up|pickup from farm|pickup at processor|how to get my order
response: For livestock, pickup is at the farm. For processed meat orders, you'll typically pick up directly from the processor once the order is ready. Dominic will contact you with details when your order is scheduled.

title: Product Availability
triggers: is it in stock|do you have it|currently available|availability|in season|out of stock
response: Availability changes regularly. The Products page shows current listings — what's there is what's available. For livestock, the Livestock page is current. If you don't see what you need, contact Dominic — upcoming inventory isn't always listed yet.

title: Live Animal vs Processed Meat
triggers: live animal vs processed|buy live or processed|what is the difference|live sheep vs meat
response: We sell livestock (live animals) and processed meat products separately. Live animals go to buyers as breeding stock or for their own processing. Processed meat (hog and lamb packages) is already done and ready for your freezer. Both are on their respective pages.

title: How Deposits Work
triggers: how do deposits work|do i pay upfront|deposit required|how much do i pay now
response: Deposits secure your place in the processing queue. The remainder is typically due at pickup. For exact deposit amounts, contact Dominic or check the product listing details.

title: Payment Methods
triggers: how to pay|payment options|do you take card|cash|check|venmo|zelle|payment methods
response: Payment methods vary. Contact Dominic directly to confirm what's accepted for your specific order. Most farm purchases can be handled by cash, check, or card depending on the transaction.

title: Scheduling Pickup
triggers: schedule pickup|when can i pick up|set a pickup time|arrange pickup|coordinate pickup
response: Dominic will contact you directly to coordinate pickup once your order is ready. For livestock, arrange pickup timing when you confirm your purchase. Call (660) 254-6226 to schedule.

title: Delivery Options
triggers: do you deliver|can you bring it to me|delivery available|delivery distance|can you ship meat
response: Contact Dominic to discuss delivery options. Local and regional arrangements may be possible depending on order size and location. We do not ship meat through the mail.

title: Missed Pickup
triggers: missed pickup|couldn't make it|reschedule pickup|pickup issue|ran into a problem
response: If you can't make your scheduled pickup, call Dominic at (660) 254-6226 as soon as possible. We'll do our best to work something out. Don't just not show — storage has limits at the processor.

title: Special Requests
triggers: special request|unusual cut|specific cut request|custom order|something specific|not on the list
response: Reach out to Dominic directly for anything specific — custom cuts, unusual requests, or orders that aren't listed on the website. He can tell you what's possible and what isn't.

title: Bulk Order Guidance
triggers: bulk order|large order|ordering a lot|group order|family order|stock the freezer|multiple animals
response: If you're stocking a large freezer or ordering for a family group, contact Dominic to discuss options. Buying a whole animal is usually the best value for a well-stocked freezer.

title: Family Freezer Planning
triggers: family freezer|how much meat for a family|feeding a family|year supply of meat|family meat supply
response: A whole hog feeds a family of four for several months, depending on consumption. A whole lamb is great for variety. Butch can estimate take-home weights and costs so you can plan your freezer budget.

title: Unsure What to Do
triggers: not sure|don't know|help|what should i do|i'm lost|confused
response: No worries — I'm here to help. Tell me what you're looking for and I'll point you in the right direction. You can also call Dominic directly at (660) 254-6226 if you'd rather talk to a person.
suggestions: Tell Me About the Farm|View Livestock|Go to Products

title: Ask Dominic Directly
triggers: ask dominic|talk to someone|real person|speak to the owner|contact the farmer|not a chatbot
response: For anything specific, call or email Dominic directly. Phone: (660) 254-6226. Email: shilohridgefarm@gmail.com or dominichanway@gmail.com. He's the one who knows every animal on this farm.

title: Best Way to Check Availability
triggers: how do i know what's available|check current availability|real-time inventory|most up to date|current stock
response: The Livestock and Products pages show current availability. For the freshest info on upcoming animals or seasonal products, call or email Dominic — not everything makes it onto the website immediately.

title: Best Way to Get Pricing
triggers: current prices|up to date prices|pricing info|how much does it cost today|pricing right now
response: Prices are listed on the Livestock and Products pages where available. For custom orders, whole animal pricing, or anything not listed, contact Dominic directly — he can give you an accurate number.

triggers: best way to reserve|how do i lock it in|claim my spot|how do i get first dibs
response: Call or email Dominic directly — that's the fastest way to secure an animal or order. Phone: (660) 254-6226. Email: shilohridgefarm@gmail.com.

triggers: can you call me|i'd like to talk|prefer a phone call|call me back|speak by phone
response: Call Dominic directly at (660) 254-6226, or use the Contact page to leave your number and he'll get back to you.

title: After-Hours Response
triggers: after hours|what if it's late|evening|weekend|not during business hours|slow to respond
response: We're a working farm — response times vary. If it's urgent, call (660) 254-6226. For non-urgent questions, email shilohridgefarm@gmail.com and we'll get back to you as soon as we can.

title: Serious Buyer Guidance
triggers: serious buyer|ready to buy|want to move forward|ready to purchase|i'm ready
response: Great — the fastest path is a direct call or email to Dominic. Phone: (660) 254-6226. Email: shilohridgefarm@gmail.com. He can confirm availability, answer specific questions, and get your order started.

title: Veterinary Disclaimer
triggers: medical advice|health treatment|disease diagnosis|sick animal advice|what medicine to give
response: I can share general farm information, but I can't give veterinary advice. For a sick or injured animal, contact a licensed veterinarian. Shep is a farm information assistant, not a vet.

title: No Legal Advice
triggers: legal question|contract|liability|law|regulations|legal advice|am i allowed to
response: I can't give legal advice. Questions about livestock transport laws, zoning, state regulations, or contracts should go to a licensed attorney or your local extension office.

title: No Admin Access
triggers: admin|admin panel|login|password|settings|backend|internal|staff only
response: I only have access to public farm information. I can't log in to anything, access admin panels, or retrieve private account or order data. For account help, use the Account page or contact Dominic.

title: Inventory Disclaimer
triggers: is it guaranteed|can you promise|is it definitely available|confirm for sure
response: Inventory shown on the site reflects current listings, but animals and products can sell quickly. I can't guarantee availability without a confirmed reservation. Contact Dominic to lock something in.

title: Pricing Disclaimer
triggers: is that price guaranteed|price locked in|will that price change|exact price
response: Listed prices are current at time of posting but may change. For a firm quote, contact Dominic directly before committing.
